title: Post-Termination Access Revocation within 24 Hours

compliance.assurance_rationale: Uplifted from contributing to partial: UC provides meaningful monitoring evidence for CIP-004-6 R4 Part 4.2.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that NERC CIP CIP-004-6 R4 Part 4.2 is enforced — Splunk UC-22.13.12: Post-Termination Access Revocation within 24 Hours.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.13.12' running on index=hr sourcetype=hr:termination, index=wineventlog EventCode=4726, archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

dataSources: `index=hr` `sourcetype=hr:termination`, `index=wineventlog` `EventCode=4726` — supplemented by authentication logs, system audit trails, and configuration management database (CMDB) asset inventory exports
spl:
index=hr sourcetype=hr:termination earliest=-14d
| eval term_epoch=strptime(term_time,"%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S")
| rename employee_id as user
| join type=left user [
    search index=wineventlog sourcetype="WinEventLog:Security" EventCode=4726 earliest=-14d
    | stats earliest(_time) as disable_epoch by TargetUserName
    | rename TargetUserName as user
  ]
| eval delta_h=round((disable_epoch-term_epoch)/3600,2)
| where isnull(disable_epoch) OR delta_h>24
| table user, term_time, disable_epoch, delta_h
description: We measure hours from HR termination timestamp to Active Directory account disable for BES personnel, evidencing the one-day removal expectation.
value: Gives the responsible team auditor-ready evidence for NERC CIP compliance by automating regulatory compliance evidence using indexes hr, wineventlog and sourcetypes WinEventLog:Security, hr:termination, hr:termination`. Replaces manual evidence gathering with a continuous Splunk search that produces structured, time-stamped artefacts suitable for regulatory examination and internal audit review.
implementation: (1) Ensure 4726 from all in-scope domains. (2) Normalize time zones to UTC. (3) Page IAM when SLA missed. (4) Optionally join CyberArk disable events as supplemental proof. Indexes required: `index=hr`, `index=wineventlog`; Sourcetypes: `sourcetype=WinEventLog:Security`, `sourcetype=hr:termination`, `sourcetype=hr:termination``.
visualization: Table (SLA misses), Column chart (delta hours), Single value (miss count). Key fields: `delta_h`, `disable_epoch`, `term_epoch`, `term_time`, `user`.
cimSpl: | tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Authentication.Authentication by Authentication.user, Authentication.action, Authentication.src | sort - count
dataModelAcceleration: Enable acceleration for the Authentication data model (Authentication.Authentication).
knownFalsePositives:
1. **Planned generation outage maintenance** — Scheduled maintenance outages on BES Cyber Systems require physical and electronic access that triggers CIP access monitoring alerts; these are pre-approved via the outage management system and documented in the maintenance plan.

2. **NERC compliance audit field activities** — During triennial CIP compliance audits, Regional Entity auditors perform physical walkthroughs, system sampling, and configuration reviews that generate access and configuration query events.

3. **Relay and protection system testing** — Periodic relay testing, protection coordination studies, and SCADA system communication tests produce legitimate BES operational signals that are scheduled and documented in the maintenance programme.

4. **Vendor remote access for approved patches** — ICS/SCADA vendors granted time-limited interactive remote access (IRA) through the jump host for approved firmware updates and configuration changes are covered by the vendor risk management programme.

**Suppression mechanism:** Maintain a `nerc_cip_exception_register` KV Store lookup containing approved outage tickets, audit period dates, relay test schedule entries, and vendor IRA session IDs. The search filters against this lookup and suppresses events where `cip_exception_approved=true` and the window is active.
detailedImplementation:
## Prerequisites

• **Splunk**: Enterprise ≥9.2 or Splunk Cloud (current). Scheduling requires a search head not under memory pressure (check `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=ProcessRunner` for OOM warnings before deploying a new scheduled search).
• **Technology Add-on**: Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Windows (Splunkbase app ID 742) must be installed on search heads and indexers. Verify with `| rest /services/apps/local | search label="Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Windows" | table label version`.
• **Indexes**: `hr`, `wineventlog` must exist on all indexers with retention matching the regulation's minimum (PCI: 1y, HIPAA: 6y, NIST 800-53: 1-3y per FIPS 199, SOX: 7y). Set `frozenTimePeriodInSecs` accordingly in `indexes.conf`.
• **Search-head capacity**: Subsearch-based detections need `subsearch_maxtime` ≥ 60s and `subsearch_maxout` ≥ 50000 in `limits.conf`. For high-volume environments, tune `[search] max_searches_per_cpu` up from default.
• **Regulatory mapping**: This detection evidences clause `CIP-004-6 R4 Part 4.2`. The control objective is: "Evidence that NERC CIP CIP-004-6 R4 Part 4.2 is enforced — Splunk UC-22.13.12: Post-Termination Access Revocation within 24 Hours." — every alert payload should include this text so downstream consumers know the compliance purpose.

## Step 1 — Configure data collection

Data collection is performed by the Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Windows (Splunkbase app ID 742), landing events in `hr`, `wineventlog` under `hr:termination`, `WinEventLog:Security`. Security channel alone produces 2000-8000 events per domain-joined host per day depending on audit policy (~2-8 GB/host/month). Domain controllers generate 10x more.

Configure the input through Splunk_TA_windows/local/inputs.conf on each Universal Forwarder. A reference `inputs.conf` stanza looks like:

```ini
[WinEventLog://Security]
disabled = 0
index = windows
renderXml = true
current_only = 0
evt_resolve_ad_obj = 1
checkpointInterval = 5
```

The following fields are authoritative for this detection once the TA parses them: `EventCode, Account_Name, Account_Domain, Logon_Type, Source_Network_Address, Workstation_Name, Process_Name, TargetUserName, Subject_Account_Name`. Verify they are extracted at search time with `index=hr earliest=-15m | fieldsummary` before scheduling the detection.


Four distinct operational considerations shape this detection's rollout:


• **Hint 1:** Ensure 4726 from all in-scope domains. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 2:** Normalize time zones to UTC. Field normalisation mismatches are one of the top silent-failure modes in enrichment-driven detections — invest in automated tests that assert key-match rates after every TA upgrade.

• **Hint 3:** Page IAM when SLA missed. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 4:** Optionally join CyberArk disable events as supplemental proof. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

## Step 2 — Implement the search

For "Post-Termination Access Revocation within 24 Hours," the aggregation summarises access activity — the control is about WHO accessed WHAT, so the key fields in the aggregate map directly to 'principal' and 'resource' for the compliance narrative.

This search correlates `index=hr` (sourcetype=`hr:termination`) on the outer side with `index=wineventlog` (sourcetype=`WinEventLog:Security`) on the inner side, joined on `user` across the last 14 days. `type=left` preserves all rows from the outer search; nulls in the inner side indicate missing data that may itself constitute a finding.

Join caveat: Splunk's `| join` subsearch is capped at `max_searches_per_cpu * subsearch_maxout` results (default: 50000). If the inner subsearch exceeds this, rows are silently dropped and the resulting report will under-count. Check `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd log_level=WARN subsearch` for truncation warnings. For inner searches expected to return >10000 rows, rewrite as `| stats` with a larger base search and post-filter, or use `| tstats` if a CIM datamodel covers the data.

Time-zone sensitivity: the `strptime(term_time, "%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S")` conversion assumes the source timestamp is in the Splunk server's local time zone unless the format includes `%z`. If `term_time` is emitted in UTC by the source system but Splunk's search head runs in a non-UTC zone, the resulting epoch will be off by the zone offset and the subsequent `where _time > term_time_epoch` comparison will produce systematic false positives or negatives. Always validate by comparing `strftime(term_time_epoch, "%Y-%m-%d %H:%M %Z")` against a known-good reference event before promoting to production.

**CIM / accelerated variant:** The detection also has an accelerated variant using `| tstats` against the CIM `Authentication` datamodel:

```spl
| tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Authentication.Authentication by Authentication.user, Authentication.action, Authentication.src | sort - count
```

When to prefer this variant: once the underlying datamodel is accelerated (verify in Settings > Data Models > `Authentication` > status=100%), this query runs 10-50x faster than the raw variant and can feasibly cover multi-month windows for audit reporting. When to avoid: if your field extractions are app-specific and the CIM tagging is incomplete, the `tstats` variant will silently under-count. Always compare raw vs accelerated counts on a known day before swapping the production schedule. Maintain both variants in the UC — the raw is the primary for accuracy, the accelerated is the performance optimisation for dashboarding.

**Schedule configuration** (`savedsearches.conf`):
```ini
[UC-22.13.12: Post-Termination Access Revocation within 24 Hours]
cron_schedule = 0 2 * * 1
dispatch.earliest_time = -14d
dispatch.latest_time = now
actions = summary_index
action.summary_index._name = summary_compliance
action.summary_index.marker = uc=22.13.12,reg=NERC_CIP
```

## Step 3 — Validate deployment

**(a) Field extraction check:** Run the following to confirm the TA is extracting every field the detection relies on:

```spl
index=hr sourcetype=hr:termination earliest=-1h
| fieldsummary
| where count=0
| table field
```

Any row returned is a field the TA claims to extract but isn't appearing in recent data — it's either a rename upstream, a TA version mismatch, or a data-format change.

**(b) Required-field presence:** This detection specifically needs: `delta_h`, `disable_epoch`, `term_epoch`, `term_time`, `user`. Run:

```spl
index=hr sourcetype=hr:termination earliest=-1h
| head 100
| table delta_h, disable_epoch, term_epoch, term_time, user
```

Every row should have non-null values for the fields the detection's `where` clause filters on. Nulls cause silent drops in the result set.

**(d) Threshold calibration:** Before enabling as a production alert, run the base search with the threshold removed and profile the distribution of `delta_h`:

```spl
<base search> earliest=-30d
| stats perc50(delta_h) as p50 perc90(delta_h) as p90 perc99(delta_h) as p99 max(delta_h) as hi
```

The current threshold `24` should sit between p90 and p99 for a balanced signal-to-noise ratio. If it's below p50, expect heavy alert volume; if above p99, expect under-detection.

**(e) Negative test:** Generate a synthetic 'should not alert' event using the Splunk Sample Generator or a crafted `| makeresults` event and confirm the detection does NOT fire on it. A detection that fires on everything is equivalent to a detection that fires on nothing.

## Step 4 — Operationalize (dashboard and reporting)

Create a companion dashboard titled `UC-22.13.12: NERC CIP evidence` with the following panel layout:

**Panel 1 — Drill-through table (`SLA misses`):** Full-width bottom row. Enable row-level drilldown to a secondary dashboard that expands each violation with its raw events, timeline, and related context. This is the panel the auditor will actually read — optimise it for scannability (avoid huge wrap-around fields).

**Panel 2 — Distribution (`delta hours`):** Shows the volumetric distribution across the primary dimension. Use `sort` so the worst offender is first — auditors focus on the tallest bar.

**Panel 3 — Single-value KPI (`miss count`):** Place at top-left. Configure `underLabel` with the regulation clause (e.g. 'NERC CIP current violation count') and set a red colour range for any non-zero value. A zero reading is the steady state for a healthy control.


Time picker defaults: set to last 30 days with named presets for 24h, 7d, 30d, 90d, and 1y — the auditor needs all five windows without retyping. Enable scheduled PDF export for the full dashboard on the regulation's reporting cadence.

## Step 5 — Troubleshooting and regulation-specific caveats

**No results when violations exist:** The most likely cause is subsearch truncation (see Step 2). Inspect `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=searchprocessor log_level=WARN subsearch` for messages like 'Output result count exceeded 50000.'

**Search times out:** Check the search job inspector (`Activity > Jobs > <this UC's last run> > Inspect`). The phase consuming the time (fetch, command, summary) indicates whether the issue is data-layer (too wide a window, unaccelerated datamodel), command-layer (expensive `transaction` or `eventstats`), or post-aggregation.


**NERC CIP-specific failure modes:**

• BES Cyber System classification (CIP-002): every NERC CIP detection must filter to BES Cyber Systems only. A general 'Windows login' search across the entire estate is not CIP evidence; a search filtered through `bes_cyber_asset_inventory.csv` is.

• Low-Impact vs Medium-Impact vs High-Impact: CIP requirements scale with impact rating. A detection that fires identically for Low and High-Impact systems over-applies controls (wastes IR effort) and under-applies audit attention. Include impact rating in the alert payload and route by rating.

• ERC (Electronic Security Perimeter) boundary: CIP-005 requires strict ESP documentation. Firewall-rule detections must reference the ESP boundary register — a rule change on a firewall that's not an EAP is NOT a CIP finding; a rule change on an EAP is.

• CIP-010 configuration baselines: baseline monitoring must use an approved baseline, not an informal one. Reference the CIP-010 attestation register in the UC; baselines without dated attestation signatures are not regulatory evidence.
grandmaExplanation: We measure hours from HR termination timestamp to Active Directory account disable for BES personnel, evidencing the one-day removal expectation. That is important for the big power grid, where proof needs to line up with what the rules expect.

controlTest.positiveScenario: Inject a synthetic test event that satisfies the detection criteria for 'Post-Termination Access Revocation within 24 Hours' — for example, create a log entry with all required fields populated to match the SPL filter conditions. Verify the search fires within the expected schedule interval, the alert populates the compliance dashboard, and the event routes correctly to the evidence index with proper NERC CIP control tagging.
controlTest.negativeScenario: Ingest a batch of test events that are structurally similar but explicitly do NOT meet the detection threshold — e.g., events with compliant values, timestamps outside the monitoring window, or source systems excluded by the scope filter. Verify the search produces zero results, no false-positive alerts fire, and the compliance posture score remains unaffected for the NERC CIP control objective.

evidence: Saved search results archived to the `audit_evidence` index with NERC CIP control tagging; scheduled PDF/CSV exports for auditor consumption; Splunk dashboard screenshots with timestamp watermarks capturing control operating effectiveness over the review period.
exclusions: Excludes test/sandbox environments, pre-production systems, development instances without regulated data, and any systems formally documented as out-of-scope in the NERC CIP programme scope statement approved by the control owner.
